narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the lowest 10th percentile, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 36, undergraduate GPA of 3.0, and full-time student status—and predicted a 54.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 10%, well below the typical student's mid-level standing around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a significant drop or stagnation in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in influence, your LSAT score of 36 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the average of 36.7 seen in successful students, showing solid aptitude there. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 also helped somewhat, nudging toward success compared to higher averages around 3.21, though not enough to offset the top concern. These three elements—Year 3 performance as the strongest drag, followed by supportive LSAT and GPA—dominated the model's decision for you specifically. While full-time status aligns with most admitted students (over 90%), and your middle income is typical, they played minor roles here. Compared to peers who pass, your low Year 3 decile stands out as unusually weak, unlike the stronger mid-range academics that define successful profiles. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps targeting a higher LSAT for even more leverage, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends—many students in similar situations succeed by addressing these gaps head-on. Schools value growth stories, especially from determined applicants like you.
